entries.definition: Toward the stern or behind a point on a vessel.
On or into a ship. The word often marks the move from shore life into shipboard rules.
A sworn statement. Ishmael borrows legal language to make whale stories sound evidentiary.
Toward the rear of a ship.
The Pequod's captain. His biblical name helps frame the novel's concern with command, idolatry, and obsession.
A valuable waxy substance associated with sperm whales and used in perfume.
A ship named in Ishmael's whaling-history account as part of early British sperm-whaling in the South Sea.
Near the middle part of a ship.
Something cursed or set apart for rejection; Stubb uses the word in the comic, foul-smelling Rose-Bud episode.
A heavy iron block used in forging metal; the blacksmith chapters make practical labor feel symbolic.
A sudden fit or stroke; older medical language often used broadly.
The elevation of someone or something to divine status.

The force a chapter creates through setting, pacing, and selected detail. In this chapter: The novel begins with Ishmael's famous invitation to know him by name, then turns quickly to his restless need for the sea. He presents whaling not as a simple adventure but as a remedy for despair, boredom, and violent impulses.

The force a chapter creates through setting, pacing, and selected detail. In this chapter: Ishmael sets out for Nantucket, the famous whaling port, but arrives first in New Bedford. The chapter turns a practical travel problem into atmosphere: winter, poverty, and strangeness gather around him before he ever reaches a ship.

The force a chapter creates through setting, pacing, and selected detail. In this chapter: Ishmael enters a strange inn full of whaling signs, stories, and unsettling art. His fear peaks when he learns he must share a bed with Queequeg, a harpooneer he has not met, but the encounter begins moving from panic toward trust.

The force a chapter creates through setting, pacing, and selected detail. In this chapter: The morning after the awkward bed-sharing arrangement, Ishmael finds Queequeg's arm around him. Instead of treating the scene only as a joke, he links it to a childhood memory of punishment and loneliness.

The force a chapter creates through setting, pacing, and selected detail. In this chapter: Ishmael expects the whalers to be loud, wild storytellers, but breakfast is stiff and quiet. The comedy comes from the gap between his imagination and the ordinary social awkwardness of the room.

The force a chapter creates through setting, pacing, and selected detail. In this chapter: Walking through New Bedford, Ishmael sees wealth, fashion, sailors, and global influence packed into a whaling town. The chapter makes whaling feel like an economic system, not just a dangerous job.

The force a chapter creates through setting, pacing, and selected detail. In this chapter: The chapel is filled with memorials to sailors lost at sea. Ishmael confronts the reality that whaling can erase bodies, families, and certainty.

entries.term: Ch. 8 Pulpit: wider evidence and trust
entries.definition: A speaker or text offering evidence; The Pulpit asks how much trust that evidence deserves. In this chapter: Before the sermon begins, Melville makes the pulpit itself symbolic: it resembles a ship, and Father Mapple climbs into it like a sailor boarding. Religious teaching and sea travel are visually fused.

entries.term: Ch. 9 Sermon: wider evidence and trust
entries.definition: A speaker or text offering evidence; The Sermon asks how much trust that evidence deserves. In this chapter: Father Mapple preaches on Jonah, who tries to escape God's command and is swallowed by a great fish. The sermon turns obedience, guilt, and truth-telling into urgent questions for anyone going to sea.

entries.term: Ch. 10 Bosom Friend: wider scene detail
entries.definition: The force a chapter creates through setting, pacing, and selected detail. In this chapter: Ishmael decides that Queequeg's supposed savagery is less troubling than many civilized people's cruelty. Their friendship becomes affectionate, funny, and serious at once.

The force a chapter creates through setting, pacing, and selected detail. In this chapter: The chapter pauses the plot for a quiet domestic scene. Ishmael and Queequeg smoke, talk, and sit together under the bedclothes while the cold world remains outside.

The force a chapter creates through setting, pacing, and selected detail. In this chapter: Queequeg is presented as the son of a king who chose to travel in order to learn about the wider world. His whaling life is not random; it is tied to ambition, exile, and curiosity.

The force a chapter creates through setting, pacing, and selected detail. In this chapter: The pair leave New Bedford and head for Nantucket. A comic travel episode turns serious when Queequeg rescues a frightened passenger after a mishap on the water.

Repeated work aboard ship; in Nantucket, routine can carry danger, discipline, or moral pressure. In this chapter: Nantucket is described as bare, sandy, and improbably powerful. Ishmael turns the island into a comic marvel: a small place whose sailors range over the whole ocean.

The force a chapter creates through setting, pacing, and selected detail. In this chapter: The Try Pots gives the travelers warmth, chowder, and another threshold into the whaling world. The ordinary meal becomes part of the book's dense attention to bodies, appetite, and sea labor.

Repeated work aboard ship; in The Ship, routine can carry danger, discipline, or moral pressure. In this chapter: The pair reaches Nantucket and chooses the Pequod, a ship decorated with the materials of whale hunting. Ishmael signs on without meeting Captain Ahab, whose absence makes him more mysterious.

The force a chapter creates through setting, pacing, and selected detail. In this chapter: Queequeg observes a ritual fast, and Ishmael responds with confusion, worry, and comic impatience. The scene exposes both his concern for Queequeg and his limited understanding of Queequeg's beliefs.

entries.term: Ch. 18 His Mark: wider scene detail
entries.definition: The force a chapter creates through setting, pacing, and selected detail. In this chapter: Peleg and Bildad hesitate over Queequeg's religion, but his confidence and Ishmael's loyalty carry the moment. Queequeg signs with his mark and joins the voyage.

entries.term: Ch. 19 Prophet: wider evidence and trust
entries.definition: A speaker or text offering evidence; The Prophet asks how much trust that evidence deserves. In this chapter: Elijah speaks in riddles about Ahab and the Pequod, suggesting that the voyage is already shadowed by doom. Ishmael cannot tell whether to take him seriously.

entries.term: Ch. 20 All Astir: wider scene detail
entries.definition: The force a chapter creates through setting, pacing, and selected detail. In this chapter: Supplies, crew, and shipboard routines gather into motion. The practical work of departure contrasts with the mystery surrounding Ahab, who still does not appear.

entries.term: Ch. 21 Going Aboard: wider scene detail
entries.definition: The force a chapter creates through setting, pacing, and selected detail. In this chapter: The chapter brings the two friends onto the ship in darkness and uncertainty. Elijah appears again, making the departure feel less like a clean beginning than a crossing into danger.

entries.term: Ch. 22 Merry Christmas: wider scene detail
entries.definition: The force a chapter creates through setting, pacing, and selected detail. In this chapter: The ship departs in winter, with Peleg and Bildad shouting, praying, and managing the launch. Ahab is still absent, but the ship has entered the ocean's authority.

entries.term: Ch. 23 Lee Shore: wider scene detail
entries.definition: The force a chapter creates through setting, pacing, and selected detail. In this chapter: This short symbolic chapter interrupts the voyage to meditate on Bulkington. Ishmael imagines him as someone who refuses the safety of shore because truth may require exposure and risk.

Repeated work aboard ship; in The Advocate, routine can carry danger, discipline, or moral pressure. In this chapter: He answers critics who call whaling dirty or unpoetic. He compares whale hunters to soldiers, points to the industry's wealth and reach, and says whalemen have helped open parts of the world that later travelers used too.

Repeated work aboard ship; in Postscript, routine can carry danger, discipline, or moral pressure. In this chapter: He extends his defense of whaling by suggesting that kings and queens are anointed with sperm oil at coronations. The tone is comic, but the point is serious: whaling supports rituals of power even while people look down on it.

entries.term: Ch. 26 Knights and Squires: wider scene detail
entries.definition: The force a chapter creates through setting, pacing, and selected detail. In this chapter: Starbuck is presented as serious, conscientious, brave, and marked by thoughts of home. Ishmael then describes how each mate on the Pequod has a harpooneer as a kind of squire, turning ship hierarchy into a knightly order.

entries.term: Ch. 27 Knights and Squires: wider scene detail
entries.definition: The force a chapter creates through setting, pacing, and selected detail. In this chapter: Stubb is easygoing, humorous, and almost unnervingly calm in danger; Flask is short, combative, and eager to hunt whales. Ishmael then names Tashtego and Daggoo and notices that the Pequod is a mixed crew of outsiders, including Pip, whose later fate he quietly foreshadows.

The force a chapter creates through setting, pacing, and selected detail. In this chapter: After many delays, Ishmael sees Ahab on deck. His scar, ivory leg, and withdrawn intensity make him feel both human and almost mythic.

entries.term: Ch. 29 Enter Ahab To Him Stubb: wider scene detail
entries.definition: The force a chapter creates through setting, pacing, and selected detail. In this chapter: Stubb lightly objects to Ahab's pacing, but Ahab's response turns the exchange into a warning. The crew is learning that this captain's authority is personal, volatile, and difficult to challenge.

The force a chapter creates through setting, pacing, and selected detail. In this chapter: Ahab tries to smoke his pipe, then throws it away. A habit associated with rest and reflection cannot survive the pressure of his purpose.

entries.term: Ch. 31 Queen Mab: wider scene detail
entries.definition: The force a chapter creates through setting, pacing, and selected detail. In this chapter: Stubb processes Ahab's aggression through a dream and a joking conversation with Flask. The comedy does not erase the danger; it shows how sailors absorb fear and keep moving.

In Cetology, practical whale facts become a way to ask how people know what they claim to know. In this chapter: He reviews earlier whale writers, argues that the sperm whale deserves top billing, and then proposes a rough scheme that sorts whales by size into Folios, Octavos, and Duodecimos. The chapter is part joke, part real taxonomy, and part confession that complete knowledge is out of reach.

Repeated work aboard ship; in The Specksnyder, routine can carry danger, discipline, or moral pressure. In this chapter: He describes the old Dutch office of specksnyder, or chief harpooneer, to show that whaling has its own rank structure. The chapter also sets up the rule that officers live aft and common sailors forward, while Ahab works inside those customs to strengthen his command.

Repeated work aboard ship; in The Cabin-Table, routine can carry danger, discipline, or moral pressure. In this chapter: Ahab, Starbuck, Stubb, and Flask eat in formal silence, with the captain's authority shaping every movement at the table. After they leave, Queequeg, Tashtego, and Daggoo eat with much more noise and freedom, while the steward cowers between both worlds.

In The Masthead, practical whale facts become a way to ask how people know what they claim to know. In this chapter: He explains how whale ships keep men aloft for hours at a time, often for months across a voyage. The height can feel calm and meditative, but it can also dull attention, and Ishmael warns that a lookout lost in thought can miss a whale or even lose his life.

Repeated work aboard ship; in The Quarterdeck, routine can carry danger, discipline, or moral pressure. In this chapter: Ahab gathers the crew and turns an ordinary whaling voyage into a hunt for one white whale. By nailing a gold coin to the mast and demanding the crew's oath, he converts labor into obsession.

Speech arranged so readers hear it as performance, not just information. In this chapter: In a soliloquy, Ahab sees his own life as a burden of iron, not gold. He admits that beauty hurts him now, then turns back to his revenge and says he will keep driving toward it no matter what.

Speech arranged so readers hear it as performance, not just information. In this chapter: Starbuck thinks through his moral conflict: he sees Ahab's madness, hates it, and yet cannot free himself from command or pity. The scene shifts from quiet reflection to the noise of the forecastle, letting the ship's divided worlds echo each other.

entries.term: Ch. 39 First Night-Watch: wider performed speech
entries.definition: Speech arranged so readers hear it as performance, not just information. In this chapter: Alone at work, Stubb turns anxiety into jokes and song. He hears enough of the captain's tension to know something is wrong, but he answers it with humor rather than panic.

entries.term: Ch. 40 Midnight Forecastle: wider performed speech
entries.definition: Speech arranged so readers hear it as performance, not just information. In this chapter: Melville stages the crew as a crowd of voices, with sailors from many places singing, joking, and arguing. The mood turns darker when the weather changes and Pip panics at the talk of the White Whale.

entries.term: Ch. 41 Moby Dick: wider scene detail
entries.definition: The force a chapter creates through setting, pacing, and selected detail. In this chapter: The chapter gives Moby Dick a reputation before the whale appears in the plot. Ishmael moves through rumors, injuries, and Ahab's history until the captain's revenge seems both understandable and terrifying.

entries.term: Ch. 42 Whiteness of the Whale: wider whale evidence
entries.definition: In The Whiteness of the Whale, practical whale facts become a way to ask how people know what they claim to know. In this chapter: Instead of explaining Moby Dick as just a dangerous animal, Ishmael studies the symbolic force of whiteness. The chapter moves through religion, race, nature, and fear to show how one color can carry contradictory meanings.

The force a chapter creates through setting, pacing, and selected detail. In this chapter: Archy thinks he hears coughing and movement under the hatches, and Cabaco brushes it off as nerves and overeating. The brief exchange turns into a rumor that someone unseen is in the after-hold and that the captain knows more than he says.

The force a chapter creates through setting, pacing, and selected detail. In this chapter: Ahab's private planning becomes visible as he tracks seasons, cruising grounds, and whale movements. His obsession uses real navigational knowledge but bends it toward revenge.

entries.term: Ch. 45 Affidavit: wider evidence and trust
entries.definition: A speaker or text offering evidence; The Affidavit asks how much trust that evidence deserves. In this chapter: Written like a sworn statement, the chapter gathers sightings, named whales, shipwrecks, and other examples. Ishmael wants readers to see that the White Whale and the fishery's dangers are not fantasy.

The force a chapter creates through setting, pacing, and selected detail. In this chapter: Ahab knows the crew still expects profit, food, routine, and whale oil. He calculates how to let the voyage function as a business while secretly steering it toward Moby Dick.

The force a chapter creates through setting, pacing, and selected detail. In this chapter: A quiet shipboard task becomes philosophical as Ishmael imagines the mat as a loom of fate, with free will, chance, and necessity crossing one another. The stillness breaks when Tashtego spots whales and the deck suddenly snaps into motion.

entries.term: Ch. 48 First Lowering: wider shipboard work
entries.definition: Repeated work aboard ship; in The First Lowering, routine can carry danger, discipline, or moral pressure. In this chapter: The Pequod lowers boats for the first time, and the chaos of whaling becomes immediate. Fedallah and Ahab's secret crew appear, confirming that Ahab has prepared more than the crew knew.

The force a chapter creates through setting, pacing, and selected detail. In this chapter: Ishmael says there are moments when life feels like a cruel joke, and whaling is one of them. After the men are dragged back aboard, he asks about the danger, decides he may as well prepare for death, and names Queequeg as the person to handle his affairs.

entries.term: Ch. 50 Ahab’s Boat and Crew Fedallah: wider scene detail
entries.definition: The force a chapter creates through setting, pacing, and selected detail. In this chapter: The narrative pauses to account for Fedallah and the private crew. Their presence makes Ahab's command feel more secretive, ritualized, and separate from normal ship order.

The force a chapter creates through setting, pacing, and selected detail. In this chapter: The crew repeatedly sees a ghostly whale spout, but the whale itself remains out of reach. The image turns the ocean into a space of pursuit, illusion, and omen.

The force a chapter creates through setting, pacing, and selected detail. In this chapter: Ishmael describes a homeward-bound whaler that looks almost ghostly from years at sea. Ahab tries to hail her about the White Whale, but the missed trumpet and crossing wakes make the meeting feel like a bad sign.

Repeated work aboard ship; in The Gam, routine can carry danger, discipline, or moral pressure. In this chapter: Whaleships exchange hails, letters, news, and boat visits when they cross paths. Ishmael treats this as one of the most distinctive customs of the fishery, and he compares whalers to other sailors to show how unusual it is.

entries.term: Ch. 54 Town-Ho ’s Story: wider scene detail
entries.definition: The force a chapter creates through setting, pacing, and selected detail. In this chapter: Framed as a tale told in Lima, the chapter follows the Town-Ho from its leaking hull to Radney's abuse of Steelkilt, the resulting revolt, and the later whale chase in which Moby Dick kills Radney. The story also shows how much of it stays secret, even after it is repeated on the Pequod.

entries.term: Ch. 55 the Monstrous Pictures of Whales: wider whale evidence
entries.definition: In Of the Monstrous Pictures of Whales, practical whale facts become a way to ask how people know what they claim to know. In this chapter: He moves through examples from sculpture, classical painting, Bible illustrations, and natural history books, arguing that artists usually get whales wrong because they work from guesswork or damaged bodies. The chapter keeps returning to the problem that a living whale is hard to see, let alone draw correctly.

entries.term: Ch. 56 the Less Erroneous Pictures: wider whale evidence
entries.definition: In Of the Less Erroneous Pictures of Whales, and the True Pictures of Whaling Scenes, practical whale facts become a way to ask how people know what they claim to know. In this chapter: He compares artists and naturalists, praising the pictures that actually catch the motion of whaling scenes. The best examples show boats, crews, spray, smoke, and the whale in action rather than as a flat specimen.

entries.term: Ch. 57 Whales in Paint in Teeth: wider whale evidence
entries.definition: In Of Whales in Paint; in Teeth; in Wood; in Sheet-Iron; in Stone; in Mountains; in Stars, practical whale facts become a way to ask how people know what they claim to know. In this chapter: The chapter starts with a painted sign of a whale attack, then moves through carvings on teeth and wood, metal whales on church spires, rock shapes, mountain ridges, and constellations. Whales become something the eye keeps finding, whether in the world or in the mind.

In Brit, practical whale facts become a way to ask how people know what they claim to know. In this chapter: Right whales feed in the brit like mowers moving through a field, but the calm image gives way to a harsher thought: the sea destroys ships, whales, and people without mercy. The chapter ends by contrasting that dangerous ocean with the small, fragile island of peace inside the human soul.

In Squid, practical whale facts become a way to ask how people know what they claim to know. In this chapter: Daggoo sees a strange white mass rising and sinking ahead of the ship, and the men rush to the yards thinking they may have found the White Whale. Instead, they encounter a huge squid with long arms, a rare and eerie creature that few whalemen ever see and live to describe.

Repeated work aboard ship; in The Line, routine can carry danger, discipline, or moral pressure. In this chapter: He describes the line's material, length, coiling, and the way it is laid through the boat before a hunt. What looks like simple rope becomes a system of hidden danger surrounding every crew member.

entries.term: Ch. 61 Stubb Kills a Whale: wider whale evidence
entries.definition: In Stubb Kills a Whale, practical whale facts become a way to ask how people know what they claim to know. In this chapter: The chapter starts in a dreamy calm, then suddenly shifts when the whale appears under the ship. Stubb stays strangely cheerful through the chase, and the crew's quick, noisy work ends with the whale killed and the real labor of handling the body about to begin.

Repeated work aboard ship; in The Dart, routine can carry danger, discipline, or moral pressure. In this chapter: The chapter describes the normal boat sequence in a whale chase: the harpooneer rows under pressure, then has to twist around and hurl the dart at the whale at exactly the right instant. Melville argues that the system exhausts the person who has to do the most dangerous work.

Repeated work aboard ship; in The Crotch, routine can carry danger, discipline, or moral pressure. In this chapter: The crotch is the notched stick that holds the harpoon ready at the front of the boat, usually with two irons waiting. If the first iron fails, the second may have to be thrown out anyway, and once it is loose on the line it can tangle, whip around, or threaten the whole crew.

entries.term: Ch. 64 Stubb’s Supper: wider shipboard work
entries.definition: Repeated work aboard ship; in Stubb’s Supper, routine can carry danger, discipline, or moral pressure. In this chapter: The Pequod tows the dead whale in the dark, and Ahab stays restless because Moby Dick is still ahead, not behind. Stubb turns the night into a grotesque feast by having steak cut from the whale, while sharks crowd the water and strip the body below.

entries.term: Ch. 65 Whale as a Dish: wider whale evidence
entries.definition: In The Whale as a Dish, practical whale facts become a way to ask how people know what they claim to know. In this chapter: He gives examples of whale dishes, from right-whale tongue in France to sailors dipping biscuit in whale oil, and even describes brains as food. Then he turns the disgust back on ordinary meat-eating and points out that supposedly civilized people also consume dead animals with little shame.

entries.term: Ch. 66 Shark Massacre: wider shipboard work
entries.definition: Repeated work aboard ship; in The Shark Massacre, routine can carry danger, discipline, or moral pressure. In this chapter: Stubb sets an anchor watch so the whale can be left overnight, but the sharks gather in such numbers that Queequeg and another sailor have to attack them under lantern light. Even the dead whale is still part of a violent struggle: the crew is working to save the catch from being stripped apart before morning.

Repeated work aboard ship; in Cutting In, routine can carry danger, discipline, or moral pressure. In this chapter: The Pequod becomes a worksite, with the mates, harpooneers, and crew all pulling together to strip the whale. The heavy body leans the ship over, and the operation feels like butchery and factory labor at the same time.

In The Blanket, practical whale facts become a way to ask how people know what they claim to know. In this chapter: He works through the strange question of where the whale's skin really is and compares the blubber to a flexible covering that can be stripped away in pieces. The chapter mixes anatomy, analogy, and speculation instead of clean scientific certainty.

Repeated work aboard ship; in The Funeral, routine can carry danger, discipline, or moral pressure. In this chapter: The whale's body floats off white and huge, while sharks tear at it below and birds attack it from above. Ishmael treats the scene as a mock funeral, then widens it into a reflection on how people build dangerous beliefs from what they see.

Repeated work aboard ship; in The Sphynx, routine can carry danger, discipline, or moral pressure. In this chapter: The detached head becomes a mute monument, and Ahab launches into one of his fiercest speeches at it. He keeps asking the head to reveal what it knows, but the only answer is silence, until the scene is interrupted by a sail in the distance.

entries.term: Ch. 71 Jeroboam ’s Story: wider scene detail
entries.definition: The force a chapter creates through setting, pacing, and selected detail. In this chapter: A gam with the Jeroboam brings news of Gabriel, a religiously charged figure whose influence has unsettled his ship. The encounter also connects Moby Dick to more injury and forewarning.

Repeated work aboard ship; in The Monkey-Rope, routine can carry danger, discipline, or moral pressure. In this chapter: Ishmael has to hold Queequeg by a rope while Queequeg stands on the whale during cutting-in. The setup makes them dependent on each other, and Ishmael uses that danger to think about how every person is bound to other people in ways they cannot fully control.

entries.term: Ch. 73 Stubb and Flask Kill: wider whale evidence
entries.definition: In Stubb and Flask Kill a Right Whale; and Then Have a Talk Over Him, practical whale facts become a way to ask how people know what they claim to know. In this chapter: The Pequod drifts into a patch of right whales, and the two mates bring one alongside after a hard chase around the ship. While the whale is being secured, they trade comic talk about Ahab, Fedallah, and whether the Parsee is some kind of devilish charm on the voyage.

entries.term: Ch. 74 Sperm Whale’s Head: wider whale evidence
entries.definition: In The Sperm Whale’s Head⁠—Contrasted View, practical whale facts become a way to ask how people know what they claim to know. In this chapter: He studies the two heads as if they were lessons in anatomy, noticing the sperm whale's dignity, the placement of its eyes and ears, and the way its massive skull differs from the right whale's shape. The chapter keeps shifting between close observation and extended comparison.

entries.term: Ch. 75 Right Whale’s Head: wider whale evidence
entries.definition: In The Right Whale’s Head⁠—Contrasted View, practical whale facts become a way to ask how people know what they claim to know. In this chapter: The right whale's head looks blunt and shoe-like at first, then opens into a mouth full of baleen, or whalebone plates, that strain food from the water. Ishmael keeps spinning out comparisons, from organs and crowns to wigs and umbrellas, to make the strange anatomy legible.

In The Battering-Ram, practical whale facts become a way to ask how people know what they claim to know. In this chapter: Melville asks the reader to look closely at the sperm whale's front end, which is a broad, boneless wall of toughness. He argues that the head is made not for delicacy but for force, and that its structure helps explain the whale's power to smash through water and objects.

entries.term: Ch. 77 Great Heidelburgh Tun: wider whale evidence
entries.definition: In The Great Heidelburgh Tun, practical whale facts become a way to ask how people know what they claim to know. In this chapter: He breaks the sperm whale's head into parts, especially the junk and the case, and explains how the upper chamber holds the valuable spermaceti. The chapter treats the whale like a vast storage vessel, but it also warns how easy it is to lose or spill that precious oil while cutting it out.

entries.term: Ch. 78 Cistern and Buckets: wider shipboard work
entries.definition: Repeated work aboard ship; in Cistern and Buckets, routine can carry danger, discipline, or moral pressure. In this chapter: The whale's head becomes a kind of cistern, and the crew works bucket by bucket to drain it. Then the labor goes wrong: Tashtego falls in, Daggoo and the others rush to rescue him, and the huge head nearly slips away into the sea.

In The Prairie, practical whale facts become a way to ask how people know what they claim to know. In this chapter: Using the language of physiognomy and phrenology, Ishmael argues that the sperm whale has no normal face to study: no nose, no readable features, only a broad forehead that looks both sublime and unknowable. He ends by admitting that human sciences cannot really decipher the whale's brow.

In The Nut, practical whale facts become a way to ask how people know what they claim to know. In this chapter: Ishmael says the sperm whale's brain is small and deeply hidden, then shifts attention to the spine and hump as possible sources of strength and character. He suggests that the whale's true power is not obvious from the outside, and that even the false brow of the skull hides what matters most.

entries.term: Ch. 81 Pequod Meets the Virgin: wider shipboard work
entries.definition: Repeated work aboard ship; in The Pequod Meets the Virgin, routine can carry danger, discipline, or moral pressure. In this chapter: Derick De Deer comes aboard begging for oil because his ship is empty, then immediately throws himself into the chase when whales are sighted. The pursuit becomes a comic but brutal contest between the German boats and the Pequod, with a badly injured old bull whale trying to escape the pack.

entries.term: Ch. 82 Honor and Glory of Whaling: wider whale evidence
entries.definition: In The Honor and Glory of Whaling, practical whale facts become a way to ask how people know what they claim to know. In this chapter: He traces whaling back through Perseus, Saint George, Hercules, Jonah, and Vishnu, arguing that the trade belongs to a long and distinguished tradition. The chapter is comic, but the argument is serious: whalers should not be treated as crude laborers when their work has deep mythic and historical roots.

entries.term: Ch. 83 Jonah Historically Regarded: wider whale evidence
entries.definition: In Jonah Historically Regarded, practical whale facts become a way to ask how people know what they claim to know. In this chapter: He collects skeptical and defensive explanations for Jonah's journey, including arguments about whale anatomy, the distance to Nineveh, and whether Jonah could have reached the city by another route. The chapter is less about proving the miracle than about showing how people argue over it.

Repeated work aboard ship; in Pitchpoling, routine can carry danger, discipline, or moral pressure. In this chapter: After Queequeg oils the boat to help it slide, the crew chases a running whale. When the whale is too fast to approach closely, Stubb shows the skill and nerve needed to throw the long lance from a distance and hit the animal while the boat is still in motion.

In The Fountain, practical whale facts become a way to ask how people know what they claim to know. In this chapter: He argues about how the sperm whale breathes, whether the spout is water or vapor, and why the animal must rise to the surface at regular intervals. The chapter keeps testing observation against uncertainty, then shifts into a more poetic view of the whale as a deep, misty being.

In The Tail, practical whale facts become a way to ask how people know what they claim to know. In this chapter: He describes the tail's size, structure, strength, and the different ways a whale uses it to move, strike, sweep, play, and dive. The chapter keeps returning to the strange mix of power and grace in the flukes, which can crush a boat or rise beautifully out of the sea.

entries.term: Ch. 87 Grand Armada: wider shipboard work
entries.definition: Repeated work aboard ship; in The Grand Armada, routine can carry danger, discipline, or moral pressure. In this chapter: Near the Straits of Sunda, the ship enters waters filled with whales, then has to push through a dense moving mass of spouts, bodies, and panic. The hunt becomes even more complicated when Malay pirates appear behind the Pequod, so the ship is both chasing and being chased at once.

entries.term: Ch. 88 Schools and Schoolmasters: wider whale evidence
entries.definition: In Schools and Schoolmasters, practical whale facts become a way to ask how people know what they claim to know. In this chapter: Melville turns whale behavior into a social lesson. He distinguishes female schools, young male schools, and the older schoolmaster whale, who grows solitary with age.

entries.term: Ch. 89 Fast-Fish and Loose-Fish: wider custom and rule
entries.definition: A practice treated as normal because people have repeated it over time. In this chapter: Ishmael explains the difference between a Fast-Fish, already claimed, and a Loose-Fish, available to whoever can seize it. He then applies the rule far beyond whaling.

entries.term: Ch. 90 Heads or Tails: wider whale evidence
entries.definition: In Heads or Tails, practical whale facts become a way to ask how people know what they claim to know. In this chapter: He uses a legal case to show how a whale, once claimed under royal authority, could be treated as property for the crown. Then he follows the logic through old law books and jokes about why the king gets the head and the queen gets the tail.

entries.term: Ch. 91 Pequod Meets the Rose-Bud: wider shipboard work
entries.definition: Repeated work aboard ship; in The Pequod Meets the Rose-Bud, routine can carry danger, discipline, or moral pressure. In this chapter: Stubb approaches a French ship carrying two bad catches: one blasted whale and one dried-out carcass that still might contain ambergris. He jokes with the captain and crew, then pulls a small haul of the fragrant substance from the whale's body.

In Ambergris, practical whale facts become a way to ask how people know what they claim to know. In this chapter: He defines ambergris, notes that it is prized in perfume and sometimes cooking, and traces the bad smell associated with whaling to older methods of handling whale oil. The chapter ends by insisting that whales are not inherently filthy and can even seem fragrant.

The force a chapter creates through setting, pacing, and selected detail. In this chapter: During a whale chase, Pip panics, jumps from the boat, and is left alone in the vast ocean before being recovered. The trauma changes him, and the ship treats that change uneasily.

entries.term: Ch. 94 Squeeze of the Hand: wider shipboard work
entries.definition: Repeated work aboard ship; in A Squeeze of the Hand, routine can carry danger, discipline, or moral pressure. In this chapter: The work of pressing the whale oil back into fluid becomes oddly soothing, even affectionate. Ishmael describes losing anger and feeling as if the whole crew should be drawn together in shared softness, before moving on to more practical details of whale processing.

Repeated work aboard ship; in The Cassock, routine can carry danger, discipline, or moral pressure. In this chapter: Melville describes the long strip of whale skin pulled over the mincer's body, then shows him standing at the wooden horse where blubber is chopped into pieces for the pots. The result makes shipboard butchery look like a strange religious office.

Repeated work aboard ship; in The Try-Works, routine can carry danger, discipline, or moral pressure. In this chapter: The try-works make the Pequod into a floating factory at night. Ishmael describes the heat, smoke, and danger until the ship seems almost hellish.

entries.term: Ch. 98 Stowing Down and Clearing Up: wider shipboard work
entries.definition: Repeated work aboard ship; in Stowing Down and Clearing Up, routine can carry danger, discipline, or moral pressure. In this chapter: Melville walks through the process of stowing the oil below deck and scrubbing away the blood, soot, and blubber above. The ship becomes clean enough to seem domestic, but the next cry for a whale will start the cycle again.

Speech arranged so readers hear it as performance, not just information. In this chapter: The coin nailed to the mast becomes a kind of mirror. Ahab, Starbuck, Stubb, Flask, and others read different meanings into the same object.

entries.term: Ch. 100 Leg and Arm: wider scene detail
entries.definition: The force a chapter creates through setting, pacing, and selected detail. In this chapter: The two wounded captains compare injuries, and the English captain tells the story of losing his arm in a whale attack. His surgeon, Dr. Bunger, joins the conversation, and Ahab listens closely for any clue about the White Whale's last movements before rushing back to his own ship.

Repeated work aboard ship; in The Decanter, routine can carry danger, discipline, or moral pressure. In this chapter: He explains how the Enderbys helped open new whale grounds, then recalls a convivial night aboard the Samuel Enderby with flip, beef, bread, and plenty of noise. From there he turns to old Dutch records to show how richly whalers were provisioned.

entries.term: Ch. 102 Bower in the Arsacides: wider whale evidence
entries.definition: In A Bower in the Arsacides, practical whale facts become a way to ask how people know what they claim to know. In this chapter: He says he learned the whale's bones from a stranded skeleton in the Arsacides, where the priests treat it like a sacred object. The chapter is partly travel memory, partly anatomy lesson, and partly an argument that the whale has to be understood in more than one piece at a time.

entries.term: Ch. 103 Measurement of the Whale’s Skeleton: wider whale evidence
entries.definition: In Measurement of the Whale’s Skeleton, practical whale facts become a way to ask how people know what they claim to know. In this chapter: He compares the Tranque whale's length, ribs, spine, and skull to the larger body it once held, then points out how much flesh, weight, and force are missing from the bones. He also treats the skeleton as something that can be checked against other museum specimens, not just his own memory.

entries.term: Ch. 104 Fossil Whale: wider whale evidence
entries.definition: In The Fossil Whale, practical whale facts become a way to ask how people know what they claim to know. In this chapter: He gathers examples of ancient whale bones, compares them with modern whale anatomy, and describes a huge extinct whale found in Alabama that was first misread as a reptile. He ends by linking whales to old monuments, temples, and prehuman history.

entries.term: Ch. 105 Does the Whale’s Magnitude Diminish: wider whale evidence
entries.definition: In Does the Whale’s Magnitude Diminish?⁠—Will He Perish?, practical whale facts become a way to ask how people know what they claim to know. In this chapter: He weighs old claims about gigantic whales against newer measurements, then takes up the harder question of whether relentless hunting could wipe the whale out. By the end, he still believes the species is strong enough, widespread enough, and long-lived enough to survive human pressure.

entries.term: Ch. 106 Ahab’s Leg: wider scene detail
entries.definition: The force a chapter creates through setting, pacing, and selected detail. In this chapter: Ahab's artificial leg has been strained by the voyage, and its repair draws attention to his injury. His body is both a source of vulnerability and a sign of the revenge story he cannot release.

Repeated work aboard ship; in The Carpenter, routine can carry danger, discipline, or moral pressure. In this chapter: The carpenter works at his vice-bench repairing, shaping, drilling, and improvising across many kinds of shipboard tasks. He seems emotionally detached and almost machine-like, but he is still a living, comic presence who can keep the ship functioning in constant emergencies.

entries.term: Ch. 108 Ahab and the Carpenter: wider shipboard work
entries.definition: Repeated work aboard ship; in Ahab and the Carpenter, routine can carry danger, discipline, or moral pressure. In this chapter: As the carpenter files and measures the ivory leg, Ahab jokes, philosophizes, and grows restless with the fact that he needs another man's work to stand. He talks about the strange feeling of a lost limb, the limits of the body, and the uneasy idea that a person can still be haunted by what is no longer there.

entries.term: Ch. 109 Ahab and Starbuck: wider performed speech
entries.definition: Speech arranged so readers hear it as performance, not just information. In this chapter: When the ship's oil casks leak, Starbuck argues that the crew must stop and repair them. Ahab resists because practical duty competes with the hunt, and the confrontation becomes dangerously personal.

entries.term: Ch. 110 Queequeg in His Coffin: wider scene detail
entries.definition: The force a chapter creates through setting, pacing, and selected detail. In this chapter: Queequeg believes he is near death and has a coffin made according to his wishes. When he decides to live, the coffin remains aboard as an object waiting for a new meaning.

The force a chapter creates through setting, pacing, and selected detail. In this chapter: Ishmael describes the Pacific as a vast, mysterious sea that seems alive with memory and dreaming. Ahab, however, does not relax into the beauty; he stays fixed on the White Whale as the ship moves toward the Japanese cruising grounds.

Repeated work aboard ship; in The Blacksmith, routine can carry danger, discipline, or moral pressure. In this chapter: While he keeps repairing weapons and tools on deck, Ishmael tells the story of how drink, loss, and poverty broke his family and his body. Perth's life ashore ended in ruin, so the sea becomes a place to keep working and keep going.

Repeated work aboard ship; in The Forge, routine can carry danger, discipline, or moral pressure. In this chapter: Ahab gathers materials and crew energy to make a weapon for the final hunt. The scene feels like a dark ceremony, with practical craft transformed into symbolic violence.

The force a chapter creates through setting, pacing, and selected detail. In this chapter: The Pequod works through long stretches of quiet weather, and the ocean looks almost gentle and pastoral. Ishmael notes that the peace does not last, but it briefly changes how the crew sees the world, especially when Starbuck and Stubb speak from within the glow of the sea.

entries.term: Ch. 115 Pequod Meets the Bachelor: wider scene detail
entries.definition: The force a chapter creates through setting, pacing, and selected detail. In this chapter: The Bachelor is homeward-bound, richly loaded, and almost festive, with a captain who does not believe in the White Whale. Ahab asks after Moby Dick, gets no useful answer, and moves on without sharing the other ship's joy.

entries.term: Ch. 116 Dying Whale: wider whale evidence
entries.definition: In The Dying Whale, practical whale facts become a way to ask how people know what they claim to know. In this chapter: After a successful hunt, Ahab sees the whale turn its head toward the sun as it dies, then reflects on how life seems to seek light even at the end. The sight pushes him into a dark, philosophical speech about death, the sea, and the forces that rule both.

entries.term: Ch. 117 Whale Watch: wider whale evidence
entries.definition: In The Whale Watch, practical whale facts become a way to ask how people know what they claim to know. In this chapter: Ahab and Fedallah speak at night about signs, hearses, hemp, and death. Ahab hears the prophecy in the most self-protective way possible.

Repeated work aboard ship; in The Quadrant, routine can carry danger, discipline, or moral pressure. In this chapter: The quadrant can help locate the ship, but it cannot locate Moby Dick or justify Ahab's purpose. Ahab smashes it and chooses his own will over a tool of orientation.

The force a chapter creates through setting, pacing, and selected detail. In this chapter: The Pequod enters a terrifying electrical storm. While the crew is frightened, Ahab addresses the fire as if it confirms rather than rebukes him.

Speech arranged so readers hear it as performance, not just information. In this chapter: The two mates argue about risk, insurance, lightning, and whether Ahab's ship is really any more dangerous than others. Their talk keeps moving between common sense and absurdity, which fits a ship trying to hold itself together in bad weather.

The force a chapter creates through setting, pacing, and selected detail. In this chapter: With Ahab asleep and a loaded musket nearby, Starbuck sees a possible way to save the ship. He thinks through the moral terror of murder, obedience, and responsibility before stepping back.

Repeated work aboard ship; in The Needle, routine can carry danger, discipline, or moral pressure. In this chapter: The storm has damaged the Pequod's compass, but Ahab turns the failure into another display of mastery. He creates a new needle and restores direction by force of will.

entries.term: Ch. 125 Log and Line: wider shipboard work
entries.definition: Repeated work aboard ship; in The Log and Line, routine can carry danger, discipline, or moral pressure. In this chapter: A practical attempt to measure the ship's speed breaks down. Pip's presence turns the moment inward, pressing Ahab toward truths he does not want to face.

The force a chapter creates through setting, pacing, and selected detail. In this chapter: After a death at sea exposes the need for a new life-buoy, Queequeg's coffin is repurposed. The object now carries both burial and rescue in the same form.

Repeated work aboard ship; in The Deck, routine can carry danger, discipline, or moral pressure. In this chapter: The carpenter treats the coffin-life-buoy as a job; Ahab cannot help reading it as a sign. Their exchange makes the ship feel full of objects that are both useful and ominous.

entries.term: Ch. 128 Pequod Meets the Rachel: wider scene detail
entries.definition: The force a chapter creates through setting, pacing, and selected detail. In this chapter: Captain Gardiner of the Rachel pleads for help finding lost whaleboats, including one carrying his son. Ahab rejects the appeal and continues the hunt.

Speech arranged so readers hear it as performance, not just information. In this chapter: Pip's attachment to Ahab creates a moment of tenderness and danger. Ahab recognizes that staying near Pip might call him back toward human feeling.

The force a chapter creates through setting, pacing, and selected detail. In this chapter: The chapter focuses on Ahab's exposed figure and the ominous theft of his hat by a sea bird. The ship is close to its goal, and even small events feel charged.

entries.term: Ch. 131 Pequod Meets the Delight: wider scene detail
entries.definition: The force a chapter creates through setting, pacing, and selected detail. In this chapter: Another ship has encountered the White Whale and suffered for it. Instead of turning back, Ahab reads the damage as proof that he is close.

The force a chapter creates through setting, pacing, and selected detail. In this chapter: Near the end, Ahab speaks with unusual tenderness about age, family, and the cost of his obsession. Starbuck nearly reaches him, but Ahab remains trapped by the purpose he has made for himself.

The force a chapter creates through setting, pacing, and selected detail. In this chapter: Ahab and the crew sight the white whale and lower boats in pursuit. The first day confirms both Moby Dick's physical power and Ahab's refusal to stop.

The force a chapter creates through setting, pacing, and selected detail. In this chapter: Moby Dick again defeats the hunters, and the signs around Ahab grow darker. Instead of retreating, he treats injury and warning as proof that the chase must go on.

The force a chapter creates through setting, pacing, and selected detail. In this chapter: On the third day, Ahab strikes at Moby Dick and is caught in the fatal consequences of his pursuit. The Pequod sinks, and the crew is swallowed by the disaster their captain forced upon them.

The force a chapter creates through setting, pacing, and selected detail. In this chapter: The epilogue explains how Ishmael lived to tell the story. Queequeg's coffin becomes a life-buoy, and the Rachel, still searching for its own lost children, finds Ishmael instead.

The force a chapter creates through setting, pacing, and selected detail. In this chapter: This long opening collage quotes religious, classical, historical, scientific, and literary writing about whales. It is not a normal preface; it is a crowded library that makes the whale feel ancient, global, symbolic, commercial, and impossible to pin down.
Ishmael argues that whaling oil may even anoint kings, turning the postscript into a boast for the trade.
The coronation image lets Ishmael connect whale oil to royal ceremony, extending his defense of whaling's dignity.
Stubb's dream turns Ahab's leg into a warning sign, mixing humor with fear of the captain.
Ahab links the setting sun to his own pain, pride, and hunger for revenge.
Belowdecks noises suggest hidden people or movement inside the ship, turning a brief night-watch scene into suspense.
The after-hold is a storage space below deck; in Hark! it becomes the source of suspicious sounds.
Ishmael explains why Ahab keeps up the ordinary hunt while hiding his real chase for Moby Dick.
The chapter shows Ahab using the regular voyage, the crew's wages, and the officers' habits to protect his secret plan.
After the shipwreck, Ishmael treats danger as a dark joke and starts writing his will.
Ishmael imagines disaster as a joke played by the universe, turning fear into dark comedy.
Whale images appear in carvings, signs, rocks, mountains, and stars, showing how often humans remake whales through imagination.
Bad whale pictures reveal how hard it is to represent a whale accurately from distance, memory, or fantasy.
A brief encounter with a strange sea creature that keeps the ocean feeling unknown.
Ishmael explains the whaleboat's first hard strike, when the harpooneer darts the whale.
The forked point in the mast where a sailor keeps watch.
Ishmael traces the odd history of whale meat and defends Stubb's meal as part of whaling life.
A violent feeding scene that turns the sea into a spectacle of damage.
The chapter uses the rigging to show how the crew controls the whale and keeps the ship working.
Ishmael argues that the whale's blubber acts like skin and keeps him warm in cold seas.
The whale's drifting body becomes a grotesque mock funeral, surrounded by sharks below and seabirds above.
A floating whale carcass can become a false sea warning, making distant ships mistake it for danger ahead.
Stubb and Flask kill a right whale, tow it back, and joke about Fedallah and Ahab.
A huge barrel used to help readers picture enormous size.
Ishmael compares the whale's broad face to a prairie-like landscape and reads it like a map.
Ishmael shows how tiny the whale's brain seems, hidden far back in the skull behind the spermaceti.
Ishmael recasts whaling as an ancient, heroic trade, tying it to Perseus, St. George, Hercules, Jonah, and Vishnu.
Ishmael recruits mythic and historical figures to make whaling seem older and more honorable.
Ishmael weighs skeptical and religious arguments about whether Jonah's whale story should be taken literally.
Ishmael treats a biblical story like a practical problem, mixing belief with comic argument.
Ishmael asks what the whale's fountain really is and explains why the spout matters to the hunt.
A small group of whales moving together; Ishmael uses the term to sort whale bands by age and sex.
The joke that the Queen gets the tail turns whale anatomy into a mock law of ownership.
Ambergris can turn a rotten whale into sudden profit, making disgust and luxury occupy the same object.
Working tackle means the ropes and gear that let sailors handle heavy whale bodies and shipboard loads.
The commercial handling of ambergris, a rare whale product prized in perfume. In this chapter, Ishmael connects smell, trade, science, and luxury.
The chapter turns rot and perfume into one problem, asking how value can come from a whale's decay.
Pip is left behind at sea, and the chapter shows how that isolation shatters his bearings.
The crew stores the whale oil and scrubs the ship, but the neat order is only temporary.
Rendered oil is packed away as cargo, turning the whale's body into the ship's profit.
Ishmael pauses over food, drink, and stores to show the long, material life of old whaling voyages.
The ship's abundance of food and drink makes English whaling look sociable, prosperous, and comic.
Measuring a whale skeleton lets Ishmael turn awe into numbers, while still admitting that the living whale exceeds the display.
The skeleton display makes the whale visible as structure, but not as the full living creature Ishmael keeps chasing in language.
Ishmael asks whether whales are becoming smaller or dying out, using fossils and records as clues.
The missing leg keeps Ahab's pain and obsession physically present.
Starbuck's warning about leaking casks becomes a fight between shipboard duty and Ahab's revenge.
The ocean seems hushed and dreamy, but Ishmael keeps the calm tinged with threat.
The sea shines beautifully, yet the chapter keeps the hunt's danger underneath it.
Ahab watches the whale die as sunset makes the scene both beautiful and ominous.
The dying sperm whale turns toward the sun, a detail that affects Ahab like a sign he cannot fully read.
Sharks circle and feed on the dead whale, turning the chapter into a grim watch over the body.
Fedallah's promises make Ahab hear danger as destiny, not as a reason to turn back.
Stubb and Flask lash down the anchors and joke through the storm while fear of Ahab and lightning stays close.
The sailors sound like a chorus as they trade jokes and fears in the storm-dark forecastle.
Queequeg's coffin is remade as rescue equipment, turning a death object into Ishmael's future means of survival.
Ahab treats Pip like a strange medicine for his own hurt, which makes the cabin scene tense and sorrowful.

In Postscript, shipboard task points to the local detail students should keep in view. He extends his defense of whaling by suggesting that kings and queens are anointed with sperm oil at coronations. This short add-on keeps the satire going and links whaling to monarchy, status, and public ceremony.
In The Pipe, local emphasis points to the local detail students should keep in view. Ahab tries to smoke his pipe, then throws it away. This brief chapter is a precise character study.

In Queen Mab, local emphasis points to the local detail students should keep in view. Stubb processes Ahab's aggression through a dream and a joking conversation with Flask. The chapter adds another warning system before Ahab states his mission.

In Sunset, staged exchange points to the local detail students should keep in view. In a soliloquy, Ahab sees his own life as a burden of iron, not gold. This chapter lets students hear Ahab's inner voice without the crew around him.

In First Night-Watch, staged exchange points to the local detail students should keep in view. Alone at work, Stubb turns anxiety into jokes and song. This chapter gives Stubb his defining tone: comic fatalism.

In Hark!, local emphasis points to the local detail students should keep in view. Archy thinks he hears coughing and movement under the hatches, and Cabaco brushes it off as nerves and overeating. The chapter is all tension and no payoff, which is the point.

In Surmises, local emphasis points to the local detail students should keep in view. Ahab knows the crew still expects profit, food, routine, and whale oil. This chapter clarifies the political problem of Ahab's command.

In The Hyena, local emphasis points to the local detail students should keep in view. Ishmael says there are moments when life feels like a cruel joke, and whaling is one of them. This chapter shows how the novel turns fear into dark comedy instead of denying it.

entries.term: Whales in Paint in: whale evidence
entries.definition: In Of Whales in Paint; in Teeth; in Wood; in Sheet-Iron; in Stone; in Mountains; in Stars, observed whale detail points to the local detail students should keep in view. The chapter starts with a painted sign of a whale attack, then moves through carvings on teeth and wood, metal whales on church spires, rock shapes, mountain ridges, and constellations. This is one of the book's clearest examples of how obsession changes perception.

In Brit, observed whale detail points to the local detail students should keep in view. Right whales feed in the brit like mowers moving through a field, but the calm image gives way to a harsher thought: the sea destroys ships, whales, and people without mercy. The chapter links whale life to the book's larger philosophy of danger and vulnerability.

In Squid, local emphasis points to the local detail students should keep in view. Daggoo sees a strange white mass rising and sinking ahead of the ship, and the men rush to the yards thinking they may have found the White Whale. The chapter expands the ocean beyond the whale hunt itself.

In The Dart, shipboard task points to the local detail students should keep in view. The chapter describes the normal boat sequence in a whale chase: the harpooneer rows under pressure, then has to twist around and hurl the dart at the whale at exactly the right instant. This technical chapter makes the chase scenes easier to read and much more physical.

In The Crotch, shipboard task points to the local detail students should keep in view. The crotch is the notched stick that holds the harpoon ready at the front of the boat, usually with two irons waiting. The chapter gives students the gear knowledge they need for later boat scenes.

entries.term: Whale as a Dish: shipboard work
entries.definition: In The Whale as a Dish, shipboard task points to the local detail students should keep in view. He gives examples of whale dishes, from right-whale tongue in France to sailors dipping biscuit in whale oil, and even describes brains as food. The chapter connects appetite to violence and exposes double standards around eating.

In The Shark Massacre, shipboard task points to the local detail students should keep in view. Stubb sets an anchor watch so the whale can be left overnight, but the sharks gather in such numbers that Queequeg and another sailor have to attack them under lantern light. The chapter keeps whale processing from feeling neat or heroic.

In The Blanket, observed whale detail points to the local detail students should keep in view. He works through the strange question of where the whale's skin really is and compares the blubber to a flexible covering that can be stripped away in pieces. Melville uses the whale's body to show how knowledge in this book is often indirect and unstable.

In The Funeral, shipboard task points to the local detail students should keep in view. The whale's body floats off white and huge, while sharks tear at it below and birds attack it from above. This chapter turns a dead whale into a symbol of public mourning, theft, and superstition.

entries.term: Stubb and Flask Kill: shipboard work
entries.definition: In Stubb and Flask Kill a Right Whale; and Then Have a Talk Over Him, shipboard task points to the local detail students should keep in view. The Pequod drifts into a patch of right whales, and the two mates bring one alongside after a hard chase around the ship. The chapter shows ordinary shipwork continuing even when the novel pauses for whale anatomy.

In The Battering-Ram, observed whale detail points to the local detail students should keep in view. Melville asks the reader to look closely at the sperm whale's front end, which is a broad, boneless wall of toughness. This is one of Melville's clearest mock-scientific arguments about the whale's anatomy.

In The Great Heidelburgh Tun, observed whale detail points to the local detail students should keep in view. He breaks the sperm whale's head into parts, especially the junk and the case, and explains how the upper chamber holds the valuable spermaceti. The chapter makes the whale's body into both a scientific object and a container of wealth.

In The Prairie, observed whale detail points to the local detail students should keep in view. Using the language of physiognomy and phrenology, Ishmael argues that the sperm whale has no normal face to study: no nose, no readable features, only a broad forehead that looks both sublime and unknowable. This chapter shows how badly human categories fit the whale.

In The Nut, observed whale detail points to the local detail students should keep in view. Ishmael says the sperm whale's brain is small and deeply hidden, then shifts attention to the spine and hump as possible sources of strength and character. The chapter keeps pushing against simple ideas of intelligence and power.

entries.term: Honor and Glory of: whale evidence
entries.definition: In The Honor and Glory of Whaling, observed whale detail points to the local detail students should keep in view. He traces whaling back through Perseus, Saint George, Hercules, Jonah, and Vishnu, arguing that the trade belongs to a long and distinguished tradition. This chapter is Melville's direct defense of his subject.

In Jonah Historically Regarded, observed whale detail points to the local detail students should keep in view. He collects skeptical and defensive explanations for Jonah's journey, including arguments about whale anatomy, the distance to Nineveh, and whether Jonah could have reached the city by another route. This chapter shows Melville mixing scholarship, satire, and biblical commentary.

In Pitchpoling, shipboard task points to the local detail students should keep in view. After Queequeg oils the boat to help it slide, the crew chases a running whale. The chapter turns whaling into exact, high-risk labor instead of vague adventure.

In The Fountain, observed whale detail points to the local detail students should keep in view. He argues about how the sperm whale breathes, whether the spout is water or vapor, and why the animal must rise to the surface at regular intervals. This is one of the book's clearest chapters about the limits of knowledge.

In Schools and Schoolmasters, observed whale detail points to the local detail students should keep in view. Melville turns whale behavior into a social lesson. This chapter deepens the book's cetology and gives students a clear example of Melville using animal life to think about gender, age, and social order.

In Heads or Tails, local emphasis points to the local detail students should keep in view. He uses a legal case to show how a whale, once claimed under royal authority, could be treated as property for the crown. The chapter turns law into a study of power and ownership.

entries.term: Pequod Meets the Rose-Bud: shipboard work
entries.definition: In The Pequod Meets the Rose-Bud, shipboard task points to the local detail students should keep in view. Stubb approaches a French ship carrying two bad catches: one blasted whale and one dried-out carcass that still might contain ambergris. The chapter mixes comedy, commerce, and decay.

In Ambergris, shipboard task points to the local detail students should keep in view. He defines ambergris, notes that it is prized in perfume and sometimes cooking, and traces the bad smell associated with whaling to older methods of handling whale oil. This digression matters because Melville keeps turning the whale into a scientific and commercial object.

In The Castaway, local emphasis points to the local detail students should keep in view. During a whale chase, Pip panics, jumps from the boat, and is left alone in the vast ocean before being recovered. Pip becomes one of the novel's most important late figures.

In The Cassock, shipboard task points to the local detail students should keep in view. Melville describes the long strip of whale skin pulled over the mincer's body, then shows him standing at the wooden horse where blubber is chopped into pieces for the pots. The chapter is a sharp example of Melville turning labor into ritual imagery.

entries.term: Stowing Down and Clearing: shipboard work
entries.definition: In Stowing Down and Clearing Up, shipboard task points to the local detail students should keep in view. Melville walks through the process of stowing the oil below deck and scrubbing away the blood, soot, and blubber above. This chapter captures the grind of whale work better than any single chase scene.

In The Decanter, shipboard task points to the local detail students should keep in view. He explains how the Enderbys helped open new whale grounds, then recalls a convivial night aboard the Samuel Enderby with flip, beef, bread, and plenty of noise. The chapter places whaling inside a larger history of trade, national rivalry, and shipboard abundance.

entries.term: Measurement of the Whale’s: whale evidence
entries.definition: In Measurement of the Whale’s Skeleton, observed whale detail points to the local detail students should keep in view. He compares the Tranque whale's length, ribs, spine, and skull to the larger body it once held, then points out how much flesh, weight, and force are missing from the bones. The chapter teaches students how Melville can sound scientific while still insisting on uncertainty.

entries.term: Does the Whale’s Magnitude: whale evidence
entries.definition: In Does the Whale’s Magnitude Diminish?⁠—Will He Perish?, observed whale detail points to the local detail students should keep in view. He weighs old claims about gigantic whales against newer measurements, then takes up the harder question of whether relentless hunting could wipe the whale out. The chapter turns whale biology into a debate about extinction and human damage.

In Ahab’s Leg, local emphasis points to the local detail students should keep in view. Ahab's artificial leg has been strained by the voyage, and its repair draws attention to his injury. The chapter keeps Ahab embodied rather than abstract.

entries.term: Ahab and the Carpenter: staged exchange
entries.definition: In Ahab and the Carpenter, staged exchange points to the local detail students should keep in view. As the carpenter files and measures the ivory leg, Ahab jokes, philosophizes, and grows restless with the fact that he needs another man's work to stand. This chapter keeps Ahab physical, vulnerable, and unsettlingly self-aware.

entries.term: Ahab and Starbuck in: key detail
entries.definition: In Ahab and Starbuck in the Cabin, local emphasis points to the local detail students should keep in view. When the ship's oil casks leak, Starbuck argues that the crew must stop and repair them. This is a key Ahab-Starbuck conflict.

In The Pacific, local emphasis points to the local detail students should keep in view. Ishmael describes the Pacific as a vast, mysterious sea that seems alive with memory and dreaming. The chapter gives the voyage a change of atmosphere and a wider world.

In The Blacksmith, shipboard task points to the local detail students should keep in view. While he keeps repairing weapons and tools on deck, Ishmael tells the story of how drink, loss, and poverty broke his family and his body. This chapter gives one of the book's clearest portraits of damaged labor.

In The Gilder, local emphasis points to the local detail students should keep in view. The Pequod works through long stretches of quiet weather, and the ocean looks almost gentle and pastoral. The chapter balances the novel's violence with a moment of beauty.

In The Dying Whale, local emphasis points to the local detail students should keep in view. After a successful hunt, Ahab sees the whale turn its head toward the sun as it dies, then reflects on how life seems to seek light even at the end. The chapter gives Ahab one of his more reflective moments, but it does not soften him.

In The Whale Watch, local emphasis points to the local detail students should keep in view. Ahab and Fedallah speak at night about signs, hearses, hemp, and death. The chapter is crucial for the ending because its riddles return during the chase.

In Midnight.⁠—The Forecastle Bulwarks, staged exchange points to the local detail students should keep in view. The two mates argue about risk, insurance, lightning, and whether Ahab's ship is really any more dangerous than others. This chapter shows how sailors manage fear with wit and technical talk.

In The Needle, local emphasis points to the local detail students should keep in view. The storm has damaged the Pequod's compass, but Ahab turns the failure into another display of mastery. The chapter shows why the crew keeps following him: Ahab can still solve real problems.

In The Deck, local emphasis points to the local detail students should keep in view. The carpenter treats the coffin-life-buoy as a job; Ahab cannot help reading it as a sign. The chapter keeps symbolic pressure high without leaving shipboard labor behind.

In The Cabin, local emphasis points to the local detail students should keep in view. Pip's attachment to Ahab creates a moment of tenderness and danger. The chapter shows Ahab resisting compassion because it threatens the hunt.

entries.term: Pequod Meets the Delight: key detail
entries.definition: In The Pequod Meets the Delight, local emphasis points to the local detail students should keep in view. Another ship has encountered the White Whale and suffered for it. This is the last major warning before the chase.

In The Symphony, local emphasis points to the local detail students should keep in view. Near the end, Ahab speaks with unusual tenderness about age, family, and the cost of his obsession. This is the strongest late challenge to Ahab's course.
A ghostly or startling appearance. The novel often makes ships, whales, and people appear as if they are omens.
Islanders in Ishmael's account who preserve and display a sperm whale skeleton.
Behind a ship or toward its stern.
Comb-like plates in some whales' mouths used to filter food. Sperm whales are toothed whales, so baleen helps clarify Melville's whale categories.
Heavy material used to steady a vessel; figuratively, anything that gives weight or balance.
A fossil whale once mistaken for a reptile; the name helps Ishmael push whale history into deep time.
A heavy object used to break through a barrier; Ishmael uses it for the sperm whale's head.
To secure a rope; in speech, to stop or hold off.
A device that blows air into a fire, strengthening the heat of the forge.
One of the Pequod's owners, a strict Quaker who mixes scripture with hard bargaining.
A case or stand near the helm that holds a ship's compass.
The metalworker aboard ship. Perth's forge gives Ahab a way to make the chase feel ritualized and fated.
Speech or action treated as insulting to God or sacred things.
The whale's thick fat layer, cut from the body and boiled into oil. Many labor chapters turn blubber into profit.
A skilled whaleman who often handled the harpoon and helped manage the boat during the chase.
In whaling scenes, small open whaleboats carry crews close enough to hunt the whale.
A harpooneer who helps steer a whale boat after striking a whale.
A ship's officer or petty officer responsible for deck work, rigging, and crew tasks.
The forward part of a vessel.
A spar projecting from a ship's bow. It helps students picture the ship as a working structure.
A rope used to swing a yard; also a verb for making ready or tightening.
Ropes used to swing or control yards and sails.
For a whale, to leap partly or wholly out of the water. The word turns whale movement into visible force.
Tiny sea life that serves as food for right whales; Melville makes it look like yellow fields on the sea.
A male whale; Ishmael uses the term while describing whale social groups.
The raised sides of a ship above the deck. Characters often lean, watch, or brace themselves at the bulwarks.
Mysterious or occult-seeming; related to cabalistic or secret interpretation.
A room aboard ship, often associated with officers or command.
Heavy ropes or chains used for anchoring and towing.
A person who eats human flesh. Ishmael repeatedly applies the label to Queequeg; the word carries sensational racial assumptions as well as plot-level comedy.
The stormy southern tip of South America, often associated with difficult long-distance sailing routes.
A rotating machine used for hauling heavy ropes or anchors.
The commanding officer of a ship; on the Pequod this authority concentrates around Ahab.
The ship's craft worker responsible for woodwork and repairs. Near the end, he turns Ahab's damaged body into another repair job.
A travel bag made from carpet-like fabric. Ishmael's carpetbag marks him as a poor, mobile wanderer.
In whaling, the large oil-containing chamber in a sperm whale's head. Melville's head chapters distinguish it from ordinary anatomy.
A long clerical garment; Melville uses the word as a startling religious analogy for whale processing.
A Roman figure associated with stern self-control and suicide rather than submission. Ishmael invokes him while talking about despair.
The study or classification of whales. Ishmael uses it to test how humans organize knowledge.
The senior mate under the captain. Starbuck has authority, but not enough to overrule Ahab.
A thick seafood stew. The chowder chapters ground the voyage in appetite, ports, and ordinary bodily comfort before the sea plot darkens.
A fitting used to secure a rope.
A box for burial. Queequeg's coffin becomes one of the novel's strangest symbols because it shifts from death sign to survival tool.
A stairway or ladder between decks.
A navigation instrument showing direction. Late in the novel, damaged and remade compasses turn navigation into a symbolic contest.
A worker who makes or repairs barrels. On a whaling ship, barrels matter because oil must be stored.
Sailors' name for St. Elmo's fire, a glowing electrical effect during storms. In The Candles it becomes an omen-like spectacle.
A citizen of the world; someone not confined to one nation or place.
A bedspread. The word matters early because Ishmael turns an ordinary bed covering into a memory trigger.
Readiness to believe, sometimes too easily.
A support in a whaleboat for holding a harpoon or lance ready.
The notched support in a whaleboat that holds a harpoon or lance ready for use during a chase.
A group of islands in the southern Indian Ocean, used here as a broad route marker.
The process of stripping blubber from a whale alongside the ship. It turns the hunt into heavy industrial labor.
A towering Black harpooneer and one of the Pequod's most imposing crew members. The narration often treats him through racialized spectacle.
A harpoon-like throwing weapon or strike; The Dart turns the act into advice about preparation and balance.
Small cranes used for lowering and raising boats.
A ship's working floor, often the stage for command, labor, and confrontation.
Beings partly divine and partly mortal; Ishmael uses heroic language to inflate whaling history.
An absolute ruler; useful for Ahab's command style.
A Spanish gold coin. Ahab nails one to the mast as the reward for spotting Moby Dick.
A biblical prophet's name used for the strange Nantucket figure who warns Ishmael and Queequeg before they sail.
The disappearance of a species; Ishmael raises the question while speculating about whales' future.
In Ishmael's explanation, a whale already attached to a ship or boat and therefore claimed as property.
A mysterious harpooneer tied to Ahab's private boat and to the novel's sense of hidden danger.
An old word for the heavens or sky, especially in biblical style. Melville uses it to make sea events feel cosmic.
The side of a body or formation; Melville often uses body terms for ships and whales.
The Pequod's third mate. He is blunt, combative, and eager for whaling work.
One side of a whale's tail. The fluke matters because it is powerful, dangerous, and symbolically charged.
The two broad lobes of a whale's tail.
The forward part of a ship where ordinary sailors traditionally live or gather.
The forward part of a ship where ordinary sailors live or gather.
A furnace and workspace for shaping metal. Ahab turns the forge into a ritual space for weapon-making.
A whale's spout; Ishmael examines what the visible breath or vapor might be.
A biblical angel's name given to the fevered prophet aboard the Jeroboam.
A spar used to support the head of a fore-and-aft sail.
A strong wind at sea, below hurricane force but still dangerous to ships.
A meeting between whaling ships at sea, usually for news, mail, stories, warnings, or trade.
An inexperienced person. At sea, the word marks the difference between book knowledge and dangerous practical skill.
The upper edge of a boat's side.
A rope used to raise or lower a sail, yard, or flag.
A hanging bed used by sailors.
Listen; the chapter title is a command to hear something half-hidden.
A barbed iron weapon thrown to fasten a whale to the boat. It begins the dangerous physical connection between crew and whale.
A specialized whaler who throws the harpoon in the first attack on a whale.
An opening in a ship's deck leading below.
A thick rope or cable used for towing or mooring.
Large ropes or cables used for mooring or towing.
An older Christian term for someone outside Christianity. In Moby-Dick it often carries religious prejudice and should be handled directly.
A huge wine cask at Heidelberg; Melville uses it as a comic analogy for the sperm whale's case.
The steering apparatus of a ship; also the place or act of steering.
The storage space below a ship's deck.
The main body of a ship, excluding masts, sails, and rigging.
A scavenging animal associated with harsh laughter; Ishmael uses the image for his grim comic response to whaling danger.
A slang word for a low, restless mood; Ishmael uses it for the sadness and agitation that drive him to sea.
A person accused of worshiping idols. In the novel, the term reflects Christian judgments about other religions more than neutral description.
Too great, strange, or intense to be put fully into words.
Impossible or very difficult to understand. The word suits Ahab's sense that ordinary surfaces hide a deeper, hostile meaning.
Island-like or narrow in outlook.
The narrator's chosen name. It also recalls the biblical Ishmael, an outcast figure, which matters because Melville's narrator often speaks from the edge of ordinary society.
