A biblical royal name used for one of the ships the Pequod meets. Its allusive weight reinforces the chapter's false-prophet atmosphere.
A triangular sail set forward of the foremast.
A biblical sufferer whose book includes Leviathan and language of unanswerable power.
A biblical prophet who tries to flee a command and is swallowed by a great fish.
In sperm-whale anatomy, oily tissue in the head, not trash. Students should watch for the whaling-specific meaning.
A brook or valley named in biblical passages; Melville uses it in the allusion to Asa and Maachah.
The central structural beam along the bottom of a ship.
A long killing weapon used after the harpoon has fastened the whale. Harpoon and lance mark different stages of the hunt.
An older term for the left side of a ship, later replaced by port.
A biblical figure associated with poverty, death, and restoration to life. Ishmael uses the name in his comic but serious talk about cold, hunger, and survival.
The sheltered side away from the wind. In symbolic chapters, a lee shore can mean safety that is also deadly.
The side sheltered from the wind.
Away from the wind, or on the sheltered side. The term helps readers follow ship movement and risk.
A huge sea creature from biblical tradition. Melville uses the word for whales, sea terror, and the long history of imagining ocean monsters.
A floating rescue device; in Moby-Dick the life-buoy becomes inseparable from Queequeg's coffin.
A phrase suggesting that one form of illness or likeness can answer another; Ahab applies it uneasily to Pip.
A tub holding the coiled whale-line in a whaleboat.
A device or record used to measure or track a ship's movement.
A simple tool for estimating a ship's speed through the water. The phrase matters when ordinary seamanship starts failing near the end.
A line used with a log to estimate a ship's speed through the water.
A post or fitting in a whaleboat used to control the whale-line as it runs out.
In Ishmael's explanation, a whale not attached to anyone and therefore available for another ship to claim.
A platform or working area high on the mainmast.
The principal mast of a sailing ship. The mast is both a workplace and a symbolic center for Ahab's reward coin.
An illness or deep disorder; Ahab uses the word for the condition Pip seems to soothe and intensify.
The desire to harm; often part of Ahab's language about the whale.
A sailor or seafarer.
A tall upright spar that supports sails, rigging, and lookout work.
A lookout post high on a mast. Whalers watch from there for signs of whales.
A group who eat together aboard ship, or the meal itself.
The supposed passing of a soul from one body to another.
A sailor who cuts blubber into smaller pieces for trying out. The job shows how specialized and bodily whaling labor is.
The mast behind the mainmast on many sailing vessels.
The white sperm whale Ahab hunts. He is both an animal in the plot and a symbol that different characters interpret in incompatible ways.
A rope tying one sailor's safety to another's work. Melville uses it to make interdependence literal.
An obsessive fixation on one idea. The term helps describe Ahab's narrowing mind, but it is historically loaded.
The Massachusetts island that serves as the novel's whaling center and the Pequod's home port.
A mythic youth who falls in love with his own reflection. Ishmael invokes him while explaining why humans are drawn to water and self-reflection.
The Massachusetts whaling port where Ishmael first arrives before continuing to Nantucket.
Long poles used to row boats, especially the whaleboats lowered from the ship.
A solemn promise. On the quarterdeck, Ahab uses an oath to bind the crew emotionally to his private revenge.
A sign interpreted as predicting future events.
Equipped for every kind of work; in the guide this kind of word needs checking against Melville's exact usage.
The ocean region where the final phase of the voyage unfolds. The name means peaceful, which Melville uses ironically as the chase tightens.
An older term for a Zoroastrian from Persia or South Asia. Fedallah is called a Parsee, and the label carries nineteenth-century exoticizing assumptions that need context.
Thin stiff card; Ahab's phrase about striking through the pasteboard mask means attacking the visible surface of things.
Another owner of the Pequod, blunt, practical, and openly rough in contrast to Bildad.
The whaling ship Ahab commands.
A ghostlike image or illusion. Ishmael's sea often blurs real danger with imagined or symbolic presence.
Ghostly appearances; Melville uses the word for fear, prophecy, and uncertain perception.
A discredited nineteenth-century practice of reading character from skull shape.
The practice of reading character from appearance; now treated with skepticism and historical caution.
A student or writer of bodily structure and function; Ishmael borrows the pose of scientific authority.
A whaling technique in which a lance or pole is thrown or darted from a distance. Melville presents it as specialized shipboard skill.
A small toothed whale relative; Ishmael mentions it while discussing whale meat as food.
The left side of a ship when facing forward; also a harbor town.
A threatening or meaningful sign of what may come.
Burial grounds for the poor or unknown; the allusion makes the Pacific's calm carry graveyard imagery.
A mythic figure associated with defiance, suffering, and stolen fire.
A prediction or warning, often religious in tone. The novel uses prophecy to blur chance, fate, fear, and interpretation.
A raised platform for preaching. Father Mapple's pulpit looks like a ship part, blending church and voyage.
A navigational instrument used to measure the altitude of celestial bodies. Ahab's treatment of it shows his rage against limits and guidance.
The raised after part of a ship's upper deck, associated with command. Ahab uses it as a stage when he reveals the real purpose of the voyage.
A biblical queen associated in 1 Kings with an idol destroyed by Asa; Melville invokes her in The Cassock.
A fairy figure best known from Shakespeare's Romeo and Juliet; Stubb's dream chapter borrows the name for comic prophecy.
A skilled harpooneer and Ishmael's closest companion.
A wedge-shaped piece or angle; Ishmael uses the term while dividing the whale's head into parts.
A biblical name associated with grief for lost children. Late in the novel, the ship Rachel searches for a missing boat.
The Muslim month of fasting. Ishmael uses the word loosely and comically for Queequeg's private religious observance, so the scene needs careful context.
To reduce the area of a sail in strong wind.
The ropes, chains, and tackle that support and control a ship's masts and sails.
A baleen whale type often contrasted with the sperm whale.
Wise, shrewd, or perceptive.
Canvas or similar material used to catch wind and move a vessel.
A real British whaling-house name used for one of the ships Ahab encounters.
A harmful nineteenth-century racial and colonial label for people imagined as uncivilized. The guide should name the prejudice rather than normalize the term.
A group of whales, especially the bands Ishmael describes after the Grand Armada.
Openings that let water drain from a ship's deck.
The officer below the chief mate. Stubb holds this rank aboard the Pequod.
The killing of sharks around a whale carcass. The scene shows violence continuing after the hunt.
A fellow sailor on the same ship.
The ship's lamps burn whale oil, so the product of the hunt lights even the sailors' sleeping quarters.

In The Lamp, working action points to the small detail that gives this short chapter its force. Ishmael describes the forecastle lit by lamps filled at the try-works, as if the ship were a small shrine. The chapter makes the connection between whales and everyday illumination impossible to miss.

In The Lamp, shipboard pressure points to the small detail that gives this short chapter its force. Ishmael describes the forecastle lit by lamps filled at the try-works, as if the ship were a small shrine. The chapter makes the connection between whales and everyday illumination impossible to miss.

entries.term: Midnight Aloft⁠—Thunder and Lightning: brief scene turn
entries.definition: In Midnight Aloft.⁠—Thunder and Lightning, brief scene turn points to the small detail that gives this short chapter its force. This short chapter holds the storm from the masthead. The chapter keeps the storm sequence moving through multiple viewpoints.
A rope or cable supporting a mast; the word can also carry burial associations.
A public painted sign; the whale-image chapters use signboards as everyday examples of representation.
A small sailing vessel with one mast.
For a whale, to dive downward. In chase scenes, sounding changes the danger and timing for the boats.
Measuring water depth, often with a weighted line.
A ruler or supreme authority; the word often carries political force.
A pole such as a mast, yard, or boom used in a ship's rigging.
A whaling officer or chief harpooneer in older usage. Ishmael uses the term to explain whaling hierarchy.
The toothed whale hunted for spermaceti and oil. Moby Dick is a sperm whale, so the technical chapters often return to this species.
A waxy substance from the sperm whale's head, prized by whalers and often misunderstood by older writers.
A mythic riddle-figure; Melville uses the word to make the whale's head feel ancient and unreadable.
An older word for melancholy, bad temper, or gloomy restlessness.
The visible breath or spray from a whale at the surface. Lookouts search for spouts as signs of whales.
The whale's blowhole, placed on top of the head rather than at the front like a human nose.
The New Bedford inn where Ishmael first encounters Queequeg. Its name points to whales and whaling before Ishmael reaches the sea.
A sudden violent gust of wind, often with rain.
A large sea creature mistaken at first for the White Whale, turning natural observation into omen.
The right side of a ship when facing forward.
The Pequod's chief mate. He is competent, cautious, and often the clearest moral check on Ahab.
The rear part of a ship.
A shipboard servant or attendant, especially around meals and cabin service.
A narrow dagger; the word makes Daggoo's sudden cry sound sharp and stabbing.
The Pequod's second mate. He jokes through danger and stays unnervingly calm.
Underground or hidden beneath the surface.
A sailing maneuver or the lower corner of a sail, depending on context.
A block-and-rope rig used to hoist, lower, or hold heavy objects aboard ship.
The rail around the stern of a ship.
A sailor; also a sticky substance used in ship maintenance.
A skilled Native harpooneer from Gay Head on Martha's Vineyard. The novel also describes him through nineteenth-century racial assumptions.
A violent storm, often carrying symbolic weight in the novel.
The officer below the second mate. Flask holds this rank aboard the Pequod.
A handle or lever used to steer a boat.
A sail or mast section high above the deck. The word signals the vertical complexity of a sailing ship.
An upper mast section above the lower mast.
A sail set above a lower sail on a mast.
Oil rendered from whale blubber, commercially central to the whaling world.
A large iron pot used in the try-works to boil blubber into oil.
The brick furnace on a whaleship where blubber is boiled into oil. It turns the ship into a floating factory.
A slatted blind; Ishmael uses the image to describe the filtering plates in a right whale's mouth.
A ship or boat.
A workbench with a vise; Ahab's body and tools meet around this practical object.
A calling, profession, or work one feels drawn to do.
A harsh word for scavenging or feeding on the dead; Melville uses it to make whale processing feel morally unsettling.
A flag or marker used to claim a whale or signal possession.
The track of disturbed water left behind a ship or whale.
A period of duty aboard ship; also the sailors assigned to it.
The long rope attached to a struck whale. It can pull with lethal speed, so Melville treats it as both tool and danger.
A small boat lowered from the ship for chasing and striking whales.
A light tackle used for lifting or lowering; in Cistern and Buckets it lowers buckets into the whale's head.
A symbolic quality Melville refuses to reduce to one meaning. It can suggest purity, terror, blankness, racial ideology, and spiritual dread.
A winch-like machine for hauling anchors or heavy lines.
Toward the wind. Directional terms like windward and leeward help students follow ship movement and risk.
A horizontal spar from which a sail is set.
Queequeg's small religious idol or household god. The narration often treats Yojo comically, which should be read as part of the novel's religious and racial difficulty.
